topicArea: Monsters, Category Trouble & What a Culture Confesses

questions.passage:
The child will not sleep until the space under the bed has been inspected, and so the parent kneels, lifts the dust ruffle, and looks, performing a search whose object neither of them could describe on request. The ritual is older than either participant and more serious than it appears. Something is being checked for, and the something has had a thousand shapes and one function. Monsters are the fears a culture agrees to give a body, and the agreement, examined closely, turns out to be one of the more revealing documents a society produces.

The word itself testifies. Monster descends from a Latin root meaning to show or to warn, and monsters have always been demonstrations: each era's terrors diagram its anxieties with an accuracy the era itself cannot see. The plague centuries dreamed of the hungry dead returning from carelessly dug graves; the age of anatomy theaters and galvanism produced a creature stitched from parts and jolted to life; the atomic decades bred giants from test sites; and the networked present dreams of contagion, of crowds converted into swarms, of things that spread. Read in retrospect, a culture's monsters are less its inventions than its symptoms, and the file is diagnostic.

Why those shapes and not others? The most durable answer is that monsters are category trouble made flesh. They congregate at the edges of our sortings, neither dead nor alive, neither human nor beast, neither self nor other, and the horror they provoke is the horror of classification failing: the corpse that walks violates the deepest of files, the hybrid insults the border between kinds, the double wears the self's own face. On this account the monster does not merely breach boundaries; it exists to mark them, patrolling the taxonomy by embodying what the taxonomy cannot digest.

The creature's employment contract, though, has always had a strange clause: we pay to meet it. Repulsion arrives braided with fascination, the theater fills for the thing the daylight mind claims to want abolished, and the standing explanation is rehearsal, fear practiced in safety, the nervous system drilled on manageable doses of the unmanageable. Stranger still is the sympathy drift. Given time, monsters migrate toward the protagonist's chair, the creature acquires a grievance, the vampire acquires a love plot, and each era's terror is eventually issued to its grandchildren as a toy, the domestication cycle running so reliably that a monster's scariness can almost be dated by it.

The practice turns dangerous at exactly one point: when the word is aimed at persons. Calling a human being a monster does real work in both directions. It names what ordinary vocabulary cannot, and the need is genuine, since some deeds exhaust the word bad and the language reaches for something beyond the species. But the reach is also an exit. The monster stands outside humanity, and to place a wrongdoer there is to declare him inexplicable, no childhood to examine, no conditions to indict, nothing to learn and therefore nothing to prevent. The label consoles precisely by exempting: if he is a monster, he is no kin of ours, and his deed says nothing about what humans, in circumstances, do. The harder sentence, the one the label exists to avoid, is that the deed was human.

It might seem that a mapped and lit world should have retired the inventory, and the blank spaces where dragons lived are gone. But the monster was conserved, not abolished. It migrated inward, to the psyche and its basements, and forward, to the machine that thinks and the code that spreads, and the containers change while the function persists, because the function was never geographic. Monsters live wherever a culture's knowledge ends, and every culture's knowledge ends somewhere.

The parent kneeling at the bed, then, is conducting something better than a pacification. The ritual teaches the one lesson the whole bestiary exists to teach, that fear can be looked at, that the space under the bed can be inspected and survived. Monsters are instruments for thinking about what cannot yet be thought directly, and a culture's monsters are its confessions, made in the only dialect the confessing culture has. The practice of examining them, under beds and in archives, is how the confession gets heard.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The domestication cycle proves that monsters are essentially commercial products whose frightening power is deliberately engineered by profit-seeking entertainment industries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the fully mapped world has no blank spaces left, monsters now survive only as children's toys and nostalgic entertainments.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calling wrongdoers monsters is the practice's one genuine function, since ordinary vocabulary cannot name atrocity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monsters are embodied cultural fears marking where knowledge ends, and examining them, rather than aiming the word at persons, is how the confession gets heard.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes one clause of the employment contract for the whole document. The theater's paying crowds and the toy cycle are the fourth paragraph's strange clause; the passage's account of what monsters are, symptoms, category trouble, confessions, precedes and exceeds the commerce.
explanations.B: This choice accepts the retirement the sixth paragraph refutes. The monster was conserved, not abolished, migrating inward and forward, because the function was never geographic; monsters live wherever a culture's knowledge ends, which every culture's does.
explanations.C: This choice inverts the fifth paragraph's warning. Aiming the word at persons is the practice's one dangerous point, the label consoling by exempting; the genuine need for a word beyond bad is conceded, but the harder human sentence is what the label exists to avoid.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage defines monsters as fears given bodies, reads them as diagnostic symptoms and category trouble made flesh, marks the single danger of monsterizing persons, shows the inventory conserved wherever knowledge ends, and closes on the ritual's lesson: monsters are confessions, and examining them, under beds and in archives, is how the confession gets heard.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that atrocity vocabulary should be expanded so that the word monster is never needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate that wrongdoers labeled monsters receive harsher sentences than those described in ordinary terms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: mark where the practice turns dangerous, granting the label's naming work while exposing the exemption it purchases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: extend the fourth paragraph's sympathy drift to human wrongdoers, who likewise migrate gradually toward the protagonist's chair.

explanations.A: This choice converts an analysis into a vocabulary program. The paragraph concedes the need is genuine, some deeds exhaust the word bad, and proposes no lexical reform; its work is exposing what the existing word does, not replacing it.
explanations.B: This choice imports a sentencing study the paragraph never conducts. The label's work is described as psychological and cultural, consolation by exemption, not judicial; no comparison of punishments appears anywhere.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph opens by locating the single danger, when the word is aimed at persons, and works both directions: the label names what ordinary vocabulary cannot, a genuine need, and simultaneously provides an exit, the monster outside humanity, nothing to examine, learn, or prevent, the consolation that exempts us from kinship. The close names the avoided sentence: the deed was human.
explanations.D: This choice drags the fourth paragraph's fictional cycle into the fifth's ethics. Sympathy drift describes creatures acquiring grievances and love plots across eras of storytelling; the fifth paragraph's human cases involve no such migration, and its concern is the label's exemption, not its softening.

questions.stem: The claim that a culture's monsters are its confessions, in the final paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a culture's embodied terrors disclose, in disguised form, anxieties it cannot state directly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: societies feel collective guilt over the graphic fictional violence their popular entertainments routinely depict.

choices.label: C
choices.text: monster stories originated in religious rituals of public confession.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cultures deliberately encode state secrets in their popular horror fictions.

explanations.A: Correct. The sentence completes an argument built since the second paragraph: monsters are fears a culture agrees to give a body, symptoms diagramming anxieties with an accuracy the era itself cannot see, instruments for thinking about what cannot yet be thought directly. The confession is made in the only dialect the confessing culture has, and examining the monsters is how it gets heard, disclosure without intention.
explanations.B: This choice hears confession as remorse about content. The passage's monsters confess anxieties, plague, anatomy, the atom, the network, not guilt over depicting violence; the disclosure concerns what frightens the culture, not what it regrets producing.
explanations.C: This choice invents a liturgical genealogy. The word's traced ancestry is to show or to warn, demonstration rather than absolution, and no ritual origin for monster stories appears anywhere in the passage.
explanations.D: This choice makes deliberate what the metaphor requires to be involuntary. The era itself cannot see the accuracy of its diagrams; a culture encoding secrets on purpose would be publishing, not confessing, and retrospect would not be needed to read the file.

questions.stem: According to the third paragraph, the category-trouble account holds that monsters provoke horror because they:

choices.label: A
choices.text: possess physical features, fangs and claws and teeth, that trigger deeply inherited predator-detection instincts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: embody the failure of classification, congregating where sortings break down: the walking corpse, the hybrid, the double.

choices.label: C
choices.text: remind audiences of specific historical catastrophes their cultures survived.

choices.label: D
choices.text: appear without warning, exploiting the startle response that safety cannot fully suppress.

explanations.A: This choice substitutes an evolutionary mechanism the paragraph never invokes. Fangs and instincts belong to a predator account; the category-trouble theory locates the horror in taxonomy, classification failing, not in threat detection.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph states the account in full: monsters are category trouble made flesh, congregating at the edges of our sortings, neither dead nor alive, neither human nor beast, neither self nor other, and the horror they provoke is the horror of classification failing, the corpse violating the deepest file, the hybrid insulting the border, the double wearing the self's own face.
explanations.C: This choice reroutes the question to the second paragraph's symptom claim. Historical anxieties explain which monsters an era dreams; the category account answers the different question of why those shapes horrify, and its answer is structural, not commemorative.
explanations.D: This choice reduces horror to startle. Surprise is a property of encounters, not of creatures; the account's monsters disturb by what they are, breaches embodied, and would do so described calmly on a page.

questions.stem: After a violent crime, an official declares the perpetrator a monster, pure evil, nothing like us, and closes the inquiry into his background. Based on the fifth paragraph, the author would most likely observe that the declaration:

choices.label: A
choices.text: correctly deploys the label's genuine naming function, since ordinary moral vocabulary simply cannot reach deeds of this magnitude.

choices.label: B
choices.text: purchases consolation through exemption, declaring the deed inexplicable and foreclosing the examination prevention requires.

choices.label: C
choices.text: will initiate the domestication cycle, so the perpetrator will eventually attract public sympathy and even affection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: fails only in its timing, since the label should follow rather than precede a completed inquiry.

explanations.A: This choice keeps the label's first direction and drops the second. The naming need is genuine, the paragraph grants, but the official's closed inquiry enacts the exit the same label provides, and it is the exit, not the naming, that the scenario turns on.
explanations.B: Correct. The fifth paragraph predicts the sequence exactly: the monster stands outside humanity, so placing the wrongdoer there declares him inexplicable, no childhood to examine, no conditions to indict, nothing to learn and therefore nothing to prevent, and the label consoles precisely by exempting, no kin of ours. The closed inquiry is the exemption operationalized, avoiding the harder sentence that the deed was human.
explanations.C: This choice misapplies the fourth paragraph's fictional mechanics. Sympathy drift moves imaginary creatures toward protagonist chairs across generations of storytelling; nothing in the passage extends the cycle to actual criminals or predicts public affection for them.
explanations.D: This choice invents a procedural remedy the paragraph does not offer. The exemption inheres in the label's logic, the monster inexplicable by definition, so a post-inquiry declaration would forfeit the learning just as surely; timing is not the paragraph's variable.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the third paragraph's category-trouble account of monstrous horror?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that audiences disagree about which classic monsters they find most frightening.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that some monsters, such as ghosts and revenants, have been sincerely reported as real rather than presented as fiction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that young children reliably fear monsters well before they can articulate any of the categories the monsters allegedly violate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Studies finding fearfulness tracking predatory capability, category-pure predators most frightening and hybrids least, dangerousness controlled.

explanations.A: This choice records variation in taste, which any account survives; the category theory predicts what kind of thing horrifies, not a unanimous ranking of the bestiary's members.
explanations.B: This choice concerns belief in monsters, not the source of their horror. Sincerely reported ghosts are still walking violations of the dead-alive file, so their non-fictional status leaves the classification mechanism untouched.
explanations.C: This choice underestimates the account's resources. Categories can operate before they can be articulated, children sort living from dead and self from other long before naming the sortings, so pre-verbal fear is compatible with classification failing beneath articulation.
explanations.D: Correct. The account claims horror arises from classification failing, so boundary violation should drive fearfulness. Ratings tracking predatory capability instead, with pure predators most frightening and hybrids least disturbing once dangerousness is held constant, would reverse the theory's central prediction and hand the horror to threat, not taxonomy.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the second paragraph's claim that an era's monsters diagram its anxieties?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Content analyses finding each decade's dominant monster traits shifting within years of documented crises, across unconnected literatures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that monster films are consistently among the most profitable genre productions across many decades and national markets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that many national monster traditions borrow their signature creatures directly from neighboring cultures' folklore and legends.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that most viewers cannot explain why particular monsters frighten them.

explanations.A: Correct. The claim is diagnostic: each era's terrors diagram its anxieties, the plague's revenants, the atom's giants, the network's swarms. Dominant traits shifting within years of documented crises, with the matching content, contagion after epidemics, machines after automation scares, and the pattern replicating across unconnected literatures, ties the monsters to the anxieties with timing, content, and independence, the symptom reading confirmed on all three axes.
explanations.B: This choice measures the market for fright, which supports the fourth paragraph's paying crowds but says nothing about whether the frights track anxieties; profitable monsters could be arbitrary in content and the diagram claim would still fail.
explanations.C: This choice documents circulation, which if anything complicates the symptom reading: borrowed creatures arrive shaped by the lender's anxieties, so cross-cultural traffic alone leaves the diagram's local accuracy untested.
explanations.D: This choice confirms an assumption the claim already makes, the era itself cannot see the accuracy of its diagrams, and inarticulacy about one's fears neither shows nor tests whether the monsters embody them.
